museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: A cold-start month for 1997: Diablo clicks into PC life, Final Fantasy VII arrives in Japan, and British players watch the Nintendo 64 approach from magazine pages.
museum.curatorNote: January is framed as a month of signals rather than a crowded UK release calendar. The strongest anchors are Diablo's early-January PC launch and Final Fantasy VII's 31 January Japanese debut.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: The year begins with two different futures: networked dungeon crawling on PC and cinematic RPG scale on PlayStation.

items.title: Diablo becomes the PC word of mouth game
items.body: Blizzard's action RPG turned clicking, loot, and Battle.net into a winter ritual. Its precise street-date history is messy, but January 1997 is the safe release window used by Blizzard-era references.
items.sourceNote: Diablo references commonly cite January 3, 1997, while some retrospective sources discuss a December 31, 1996 availability claim. Kept as January 1997.

items.title: Final Fantasy VII launches in Japan
items.body: Square's PlayStation epic arrived in Japan, immediately becoming an import-shop object and a magazine-screen mystery for Western players.
items.sourceNote: Final Fantasy VII references consistently list Japan as January 31, 1997.

items.title: The UK waits for the Nintendo 64
items.body: The N64 was already a known object abroad, but in Britain it still belonged to preview pages, price talk, and arguments about cartridges before its March European launch.
items.sourceNote: Nintendo 64 launched in Europe on March 1, 1997; January is pre-launch UK context.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: A short shelf, but a loud one: January's biggest releases cast long shadows across the rest of the year.

items.title: Diablo
items.body: A dark, compulsive PC dungeon whose loot loop and online support helped define Blizzard's year.
items.sourceNote: Marked by month because release-date sources conflict around January 3 versus late-1996 availability.

items.title: Final Fantasy VII
items.body: Japanese release only, but already a Western event through screenshots, imports, and the sense that RPG scale had changed.

items.title: Nintendo 64 launch library previews
items.body: Super Mario 64, Pilotwings 64, and Star Wars: Shadows of the Empire were not new globally, but they were about to become the British launch shelf.
items.sourceNote: Listed as UK pre-launch context, not a January UK release.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: January feels like a hardware forecast: modems, PlayStations, and the not-yet-launched European N64.

items.title: Battle.net and the household phone line
items.body: Diablo made online play feel close enough to touch, but in UK homes the internet was still scheduled around phone access and patience.
items.sourceNote: Battle.net launched at the end of 1996; Diablo popularised it in early 1997.

items.title: N64 anticipation before launch
items.body: The machine's analogue stick and cartridge format were already talking points before British players could buy one officially.

sections.title: Magazine Covers
sections.subtitle: The newsagent shelf did what broadband could not: show the future before it arrived.

items.title: UK PlayStation press
items.headline: Final Fantasy VII and the import imagination
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed PlayStation magazine-style cover image standing in for January 1997.
items.curatorNote: A placeholder for the way PlayStation magazines made Japanese screenshots feel like museum glass: close, bright, and not yet playable.
items.body: A placeholder for the way PlayStation magazines made Japanese screenshots feel like museum glass: close, bright, and not yet playable.
items.sourceNote: Specific January cover lines are not asserted; included as press context.

items.title: N64 launch previews
items.headline: The European N64 waiting room
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed N64 magazine-style cover image standing in for January 1997.
items.curatorNote: Before the console reached UK shops, the magazine page was the place where Mario 64 became a promise.
items.body: Before the console reached UK shops, the magazine page was the place where Mario 64 became a promise.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: A month where online play was still a deliberate act.

items.title: Diablo made logging on feel purposeful
items.body: Battle.net meant the dungeon was no longer entirely private. Connecting was noisy, slow, and somehow futuristic.

items.title: Import knowledge travelled as text
items.body: Final Fantasy VII information moved through fan pages, forums, and printed magazine captions long before most Western players saw a disc.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: January 1997 as a waiting room with one very dark PC in the corner.

items.title: The year opened underground
items.body: Diablo made the PC feel like a cellar door: one more run, one more item, one more room below.

items.title: The future was printed before it was playable
items.body: For UK players, Final Fantasy VII and the N64 were often images first, purchases later.

sources.label: Diablo release context
sources.note: Used for January 1997 release context while marking exact-day uncertainty.

sources.label: Final Fantasy VII release context
sources.note: Used for the January 31, 1997 Japanese release.

sources.label: Nintendo 64 European launch context
sources.note: Used for Europe launch timing and launch-game context.
